content:
The Boolean expression

$$ \sim(p \wedge q) \vee(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge \sim q) $$

is equivalent to
explanation:
Using DeMorgan's law,

$$ \sim(p \wedge q)=\sim p \vee \sim q $$

Thus, our expression becomes

$$ (\sim p \vee \sim q) \vee(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge \sim q) $$

Rearrange and group similar terms

$$ \sim p \vee(\sim q \vee(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge \sim q)) $$

Since, $\sim q \vee(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge \sim q)$ simplifies to $\sim q \vee p$, we get

$$ \sim p \vee q $$

The Boolean expression simplifies to $\sim p \vee q$.

content: The Boolean expression $ \sim(p \vee q) \vee(\sim p \wedge q) $ is equivalent to
explanation:
The Boolean expression $ \sim(p \vee q) \vee (\sim p \wedge q) $ is simplified as follows:

Start with the given expression:

$ \sim(p \vee q) \vee (\sim p \wedge q) $

Apply De Morgan's Law:

$ (\sim p \wedge \sim q) \vee (\sim p \wedge q) $

Use the Distributive Law:

$ \sim p \wedge (\sim q \vee q) $

By the Complement Law, $(\sim q \vee q)$ is a tautology ($t$):

$ \sim p \wedge t $

Finally, apply the Identity Law:

$ \sim p $

Therefore, the expression simplifies to $ \sim p $.

content: Which of the following is always true?
explanation:
Since, $$\sim$$ (p $$\vee$$ q) $$\equiv$$ ($$\sim$$ p $$\wedge$$ $$\sim$$ q) and $$\sim$$ (p $$\wedge$$ q) $$\equiv$$ ($$\sim$$ p $$\vee$$ $$\sim$$ q)

So, options (a) and (d) are not true.

$$\sim$$ (p $$\to$$ q) $$\equiv$$ p $$\wedge$$ $$\sim$$ q, so option (c) is not true.

Now, p $$\to$$ q $$\sim$$ p $$\vee$$ q

$$\sim$$ q $$\to$$ $$\sim$$ p $$\equiv$$ [$$\sim$$ ($$\sim$$ q) $$\vee$$ $$\sim$$ p]

$$\equiv$$ q $$\vee$$ $$\sim$$ p $$\equiv$$ p $$\vee$$ q $$\Rightarrow$$ p $$\to$$ q $$\equiv$$ ($$\sim$$ q $$\to$$ $$\sim$$ p)
